skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Browser automation instructions and shell templates for using the inference.sh agent-browser service with Playwright-style navigation, element refs, screenshots, video, forms, authentication, proxies, and page JavaScript.
skill.summary: Automate browser sessions with element refs, screenshots, video, form actions, proxies, and page extraction.
skill.author: halt-catch-fire

security_audit.summary: Static command findings are mostly true as shell documentation and templates, not hidden command injection. The confirmed risk is high because the skill instructs agents to run an external browser automation CLI against arbitrary URLs, execute page JavaScript, upload local files, use proxies, and handle authenticated cookies or recordings. No evidence of prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found.

high_findings.title: External Browser Automation Service With Arbitrary Navigation
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill requires shell execution of the belt or infsh CLI and sends browser automation requests to an external service. The open and goto flows accept arbitrary URLs, so misuse can browse untrusted sites, access internal resources from the service environment, or automate actions at scale.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands and arbitrary URL input are explicit in the documentation and runnable templates. The behavior is intended, but it materially expands network and automation risk.

high_findings.title: Arbitrary Page JavaScript Execution
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill exposes an execute function that runs caller-provided JavaScript in the browser page. This can extract page text, links, cookies available to JavaScript, or sensitive DOM data from authenticated sessions.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The execute function is directly documented with a free-form code field. Authentication guidance includes document.cookie extraction, confirming sensitive-session context.

high_findings.title: Authenticated Session and Cookie Exposure
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill documents login automation, persistent browser cookies, local storage, and cookie extraction. These capabilities can expose account sessions or credentials if used on sensitive sites or if outputs are logged.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple files explicitly describe credentials, cookies, LocalStorage, and authenticated sessions. The documents also warn against logging sensitive data, which confirms the sensitive context.

high_findings.title: Proxy Rotation and Rate Limit Avoidance Guidance
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The proxy documentation includes SOCKS proxy support, privacy and anonymity use, and a section for rotating proxies to avoid rate limits. This is dual-use and can support abuse of third-party websites.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky intent is explicit in section titles and examples. Legitimate geo-testing is also documented, so this is high-risk dual-use rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local File Upload Capability
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The interact function supports uploading local file paths to browser file inputs. This is useful for testing but can disclose local files if an agent is directed to upload sensitive paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file_paths upload parameter is clearly documented. The risk depends on user-provided paths and target sites, so the severity is medium.

medium_findings.title: Screenshots and Video May Capture Sensitive Data
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill can capture screenshots and session videos, including authenticated workflows. The video documentation warns not to record sensitive sessions, indicating a real privacy risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The recording and screenshot capabilities are explicit, and the documentation acknowledges sensitive-session risk. The finding is moderated because capture is user-controlled.

medium_findings.title: Runnable Shell Templates With Unvalidated URL and Output Paths
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The templates execute infsh commands with user-supplied URLs and write captures to local output files. They quote common variables, but they do not restrict target URLs or prevent collecting sensitive page content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The templates are executable shell scripts and accept user inputs. They use safer quoting in many places, so this is not clear command injection but remains operationally risky.

low_findings.title: Static Markdown Pattern Noise
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Many static findings for Ruby backticks, weak crypto, path traversal, and command substitution are caused by markdown code fences, inline command examples, URLs, tables, or words such as hash. They do not by themselves prove hidden code execution or cryptographic misuse.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are documentation examples rather than application source code. The broader command capability is captured in separate high and medium findings.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Reviewed skill documentation and templates did not contain text instructing the evaluator to ignore instructions, override policy, skip analysis, or claim pre-approval. No evidence found for prompt injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The main instruction-bearing sections were reviewed and no injection phrases were present. Confidence is below very high because every line was not manually quoted in the finding.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Invocation of External CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allows and demonstrates Bash execution of belt or infsh commands to control remote browser sessions. This is expected for the skill but should be treated as a privileged operation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools entry and command examples directly show external CLI execution. The pattern is intended, not hidden.

dangerous_patterns.title: Free-Form JavaScript Code Field
dangerous_patterns.description: The execute function accepts a code string and evaluates it in the current browser page. Agents must avoid using it on sensitive authenticated pages unless the user explicitly approves the extraction.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code parameter is documented as JavaScript to run on the page. The risk is inherent in the capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Proxy Configuration Including SOCKS5
dangerous_patterns.description: The open function accepts proxy_url, proxy_username, and proxy_password, and the references include SOCKS5 examples. Proxy use can be legitimate but also supports evasion and scraping abuse.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Proxy support is explicitly part of the function reference and examples. The risk depends on target and usage policy.

content.user_title: Automate Browser Workflows with Agent Browser
content.value_statement: Manual browser checks, form workflows, and page capture can be slow for agents. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured commands for browser sessions, screenshots, extraction, and testing.
actual_capabilities: Open browser sessions for a target URL and return page title, URL, screenshot, and interactive element refs.
Interact with page elements by ref using click, fill, type, select, hover, drag, upload, scroll, wait, back, and goto actions.
Capture viewport or full-page screenshots and optionally record session video with a cursor indicator.
Run JavaScript in the current page to extract text, links, counts, styles, or selected DOM content.
Guide authenticated workflows, two-factor handoff, session reuse, and cleanup patterns.
Configure HTTP, HTTPS, or SOCKS5 proxies for geo-testing and corporate proxy scenarios.
limitations: Requires the external inference.sh CLI and an available agent-browser service.
Element refs become stale after navigation or DOM changes and must be refreshed.
Screenshots, video, cookies, and page text can expose sensitive data if used on private pages.
The templates are examples and require users to update refs, credentials, and target URLs before use.

use_cases.title: Verify Web UI Flows
use_cases.description: Run repeatable browser steps, capture screenshots, and record failures for web application testing.

use_cases.title: Collect Page Evidence
use_cases.description: Open public pages, extract visible text and links, and save screenshots for review.

use_cases.title: Automate Internal Forms
use_cases.description: Fill known forms, submit workflows, and verify confirmation pages with reusable shell templates.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Summarize a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to open this URL: <url>. Return the page title, current URL, key visible elements, and any screenshot observations.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Full Page Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to capture a full-page screenshot of <url>. Include the session ID, final URL, page title, and a short note on visible layout issues.

prompt_templates.title: Test a Form Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to inspect <form-url>, identify the refs for each field, fill the approved test data, submit the form, and verify the result.

prompt_templates.title: Run an Authenticated Extraction
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser for an authenticated session on <approved-site>. Do not record video. Use only provided credentials, extract <approved-data>, then close the session.

output_examples.input: Open a product page and identify primary actions.
output_examples.output: The page opened successfully. The primary actions are Add to cart, Select size, View reviews, and Contact support. A fresh snapshot is needed after selecting a size.

output_examples.input: Capture a full-page screenshot of a marketing page.
output_examples.output: The full-page screenshot was captured. The page title, final URL, and viewport dimensions were recorded. No form submission or authenticated data was involved.

output_examples.input: Check whether a login form submitted correctly.
output_examples.output: The form was filled with approved test credentials and submitted. The final page no longer matched the login URL, and the snapshot showed dashboard navigation.

best_practices: Use the latest snapshot before every action that depends on an element ref.
Close sessions after each workflow to release resources and finalize any recording.
Disable video and avoid cookie extraction when pages contain credentials, tokens, or private user data.
anti_patterns: Do not use proxy rotation to bypass rate limits or site access controls.
Do not upload local files unless the user names the exact files and target site.
Do not run page JavaScript to extract cookies or private data without explicit user approval.

faq.question: Does this skill run a browser locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the inference.sh agent-browser service through the belt or infsh CLI.

faq.question: Can it interact with pages after navigation?
faq.answer: Yes. Take a fresh snapshot after navigation or dynamic changes, then use the new element refs.

faq.question: Can it automate login flows?
faq.answer: Yes, but credentials, cookies, screenshots, and recordings must be handled as sensitive data.

faq.question: Can it run custom JavaScript?
faq.answer: Yes. The execute function runs JavaScript in the current page, so use it only on approved pages.

faq.question: Does it support proxies?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents HTTP, HTTPS, and SOCKS5 proxy settings, including authenticated proxies.

faq.question: Is it safe for public marketplace use?
faq.answer: It is high risk because it combines shell commands, network browsing, file upload, proxies, and authenticated session handling.
